Subject: Contact info & visit schedule for shut‑in list

Hi Sarah,

Thanks again for taking on the coordination piece for the homebound and assisted‑living visits. With work and trips to Kentwood to help my dad after his stroke, my schedule has been a little unpredictable, so it will really help to have you centralizing everything.

Here’s what makes the most sense for me this spring:

- I can usually do one visit during the week (Mon or Thu late afternoon) and one on a Saturday morning each month.
- I’m already visiting Jim and Carol at Willow Ridge the first Saturday of the month, so you can keep me assigned there unless something changes.
- I’m happy to rotate in for hospital calls when Pastor is out of town, but I’ll need at least a day’s notice so I can shift things at work.

Use my cell for anything time‑sensitive or last‑minute changes: (248) 555-2394. That’s the best way to reach me if I’m on the shop floor or driving back from Kentwood. Otherwise, email is fine for the regular schedule.

Also, if you’re putting together a master list, you can just list me as “Michael Peterson – Deacon (Word & Service).” I’m usually at the 8:15 a.m. Bible class setup on Sundays if someone needs to track me down in person.

Let me know if you’d like to sit down after church sometime to sketch out March and April.

In Christ,
Michael

Hey Em,

Good morning,

As part of our annual safety and emergency preparedness review, we are updating the employee contact directory. Please review the information listed below for accuracy and reply with any corrections by Friday, November 22.

Name: Michael A. Peterson
Department: Engineering / Operations

Current records show the following contact numbers for you:

Home phone: 248-555-0187

We do not list personal cell numbers in the public directory, but we strongly encourage you to provide at least one off-site contact number (spouse, home, or alternate mobile) for use in case of emergency plant closure, medical incident, or severe weather. If you wish to add or update an emergency-only personal number (e.g., your own mobile or your spouse’s), please include it clearly labeled in your reply, such as:

If no changes are required, simply reply “OK as listed.” If you have questions or would prefer to verify by phone, you may call HR at 248-555-3300 during normal business hours.

Best regards,

Kathy Miller

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Peterson <michael.peterson@gmail.com>
To: Sarah Klein <sarah.klein@gracelutherannovi.org>
Date: 2026-02-27 21:14
Subject: Bio for Adult Bible Study & Deacon Page

Hi Sarah,

Sorry for the slow response—this week has been a bit of a blur between a line‑down issue at the plant and shuttling Nathan to soccer and band. Thanks for pulling together the updated bios for the church website and the adult Bible study handout. Here’s some text you can use for mine; feel free to edit for length or tone:

“Michael Andrew Peterson has been a member of Grace Lutheran since 2008 and currently serves as a deacon and adult Bible study leader. He and his wife, Laura, live in Novi and have three children: Emily (medical student in Chicago), Jacob (senior at Michigan State), and Nathan (sophomore at Novi High School). Michael works as a Senior Mechanical Engineer / Project Manager at Midwest Tooling & Automation in Farmington Hills, where he oversees a small engineering team and coordinates custom automation projects for automotive suppliers. In his free time, he enjoys running at Lakeshore Park, reading theology, and visiting his parents in Kentwood.”

If that’s too long for the bulletin, maybe just keep the first three sentences and the job line, and cut the hobbies. I’m fine either way.

Also, Pastor mentioned you might add a short note about our new homebound communion rotation. If you want to say I help coordinate visits, that’s accurate, but don’t make it sound like I do it all myself—the team really has stepped up.

Let me know if you need a photo that’s more recent than the one from Emily’s high school graduation. I can ask Jacob to take a quick picture when he’s home over spring break.

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Peterson <m.peterson73@gmail.com>
To: Laura Peterson <lauracpeterson@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-01-06 21:14
Subject: That LinkedIn message about the Livonia position

Laura,

Forwarding you the job description I mentioned at dinner. The recruiter keeps nudging me about this "Plant Engineering Manager" role with Great Lakes Precision Components over in Livonia.

They’re emphasizing the mix of hands-on mechanical work and operations oversight, which sounds a lot like what I already do, just with a different title. It’s framed as a step up from a senior engineer into something closer to a plant manager / operations director hybrid. Salary range looks decent, and they’re talking about a 10–15% bonus tied to production metrics.

My hesitation is the same as what I said tonight: I’m not sure I want to jump from one high-pressure environment into another. Plus, I keep thinking about the parents’ support group at church on the first Thursdays. Since 2022 I’ve quietly coordinated that group for parents of teens with anxiety and depression, and Thursday evenings are pretty locked in for me now. If I took on a bigger role, I worry I’d start missing those meetings or be too drained to follow up with folks between sessions.

I also don’t want a repeat of when I tried serving on church council and ended up resigning halfway through my first term because I was simply burned out. I’ve learned I need to be realistic about my capacity.

Still, part of me wonders if I should at least take the screening call. Can we talk more about it tomorrow night after Nathan’s practice?

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: That LinkedIn message about the Livonia position

artifacts.content:
From: HR Department <careers@glpcorp.com>
To: Michael Peterson <m.peterson73@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-01-07 09:32
Subject: Re: Application – Plant Engineering Manager (Req #PEM-2417)

Dear Michael,

Thank you for your interest in the Plant Engineering Manager position (Req #PEM-2417) at Great Lakes Precision Components in Livonia, MI.

We have reviewed your background in mechanical engineering and operations oversight and believe your experience aligns well with what we’re seeking for this leadership role. The Plant Engineering Manager reports directly to the Director of Manufacturing and is responsible for supervising a team of 6–8 engineers and maintenance technicians, managing capital projects, and driving continuous improvement initiatives on the production floor.

As discussed in our phone screen, this is a full-time, on-site position with typical hours of 7:30 a.m.–5:00 p.m., Monday through Friday, with occasional weekend coverage during major equipment installs. The role is exempt and includes eligibility for our annual performance bonus program.

Next Steps:
- We would like to invite you to a virtual interview with the Director of Manufacturing and the HR Business Partner on Thursday, January 16 at 3:00 p.m. ET.
- Please confirm your availability.
- Prior to the interview, reply with an updated resume clearly listing your current title (e.g., Engineering Manager, Operations Lead, or similar) and a brief summary of your supervisory responsibilities.

We understand you are also active in your church community and have evening commitments, so we will do our best to accommodate your schedule for subsequent rounds.

We look forward to hearing from you.

Sincerely,

Amanda Lewis
Senior Talent Acquisition Partner
Great Lakes Precision Components

metadata.subject: Re: Application – Plant Engineering Manager (Req #PEM-2417)

artifacts.content:
Title: Coffee with Mark – talk about engineering manager roles
Date: 2025-02-11 07:30-08:30
Location: Biggby Coffee – Novi (Grand River Ave)
Attendees: Michael Peterson; Mark Jensen

Notes:

Catch up with Mark about his transition from "Senior Manufacturing Engineer" into the Engineering Manager role at MetroTech Systems. I want to pick his brain on how he knew it was time to move from a pure engineering position into something more like an operations or plant management track.

Questions to ask Mark:
- How did you explain your current occupation on resumes and LinkedIn when you were in that in-between stage (part technical lead, part production coordinator)?
- What parts of your job now feel most different from being a senior engineer?
- How do you protect your evenings? I’ve got the parents-of-teens support group at church the first Thursday of every month from 7:00–8:30 p.m., and I don’t want a new job to steamroll that.
- Any regrets about stepping away from some volunteer roles? I remember how overwhelmed I felt when I was on church council and ended up resigning mid-term. Don’t want to repeat that.

Bring:
- Printed copy of my current resume (shows mix of mechanical design work and production oversight).
- Rough list of what I enjoy vs. what drains me in my present role.

Reminder: leave by 8:30 sharp to get to the office before the 9:00 a.m. production meeting.

metadata.title: Coffee with Mark – talk about engineering manager roles

artifacts.content:
From: Pastor Jonathan Keller <jkeller@gracelutherannovi.org>
To: Michael Peterson <m.peterson73@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-11-18 13:47
Subject: Quick reference for your deacon paperwork

Hi Michael,

Per your request, here’s the brief summary I’ve been using when I describe your vocation and congregational roles for district forms and reference letters.

"Michael Peterson is a seasoned mechanical engineer with substantial experience in production oversight and team coordination in the automotive supply sector. In his daily work, he functions in a hybrid capacity that blends technical engineering responsibilities with operations leadership, including supervising staff, coordinating schedules, and troubleshooting issues on the shop floor."

On the church side, I typically write something like:

"At Grace Lutheran, Michael serves as a deacon (Word and Service). He assists with congregational care, hospital and shut-in visits, and leads the Sunday morning adult Bible study. Since 2022 he has also quietly coordinated a small support group for parents of teens dealing with anxiety and depression, meeting monthly in the church library. He maintains a confidential contact list and follows up with parents between meetings. Michael previously served on the church council but stepped down mid-term when he discerned that the time commitment, combined with other responsibilities, was leading to burnout."

Feel free to adjust wording as needed for your deacon training portfolio. If the seminary needs a more formal employment description, I can expand the section about your current position in the automotive industry and emphasize the supervisory aspects.

Peace in Christ,
Pastor Keller

metadata.subject: Quick reference for your deacon paperwork

artifacts.content:
Title: Update resume & LinkedIn – finalize job title wording

Attendees: Michael Peterson

Description:

Block time to clean up my resume and LinkedIn profile before the Great Lakes Precision Components interview.

To do:
- Decide how to phrase current occupation so it reflects both engineering and operations responsibilities without locking me into one narrow title.
  * Options to test: "Engineering Operations Manager," "Mechanical Engineering Supervisor," or "Manufacturing Engineering Lead." Avoid anything that sounds too inflated but still shows I’m more than just a staff engineer.

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Peterson <michael.peterson@gmail.com>
To: Laura Peterson <laura.peterson@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-01-06 21:17
Subject: That recruiter call during dinner

Laura,

Sorry again that I took that call right in the middle of Nathan telling us about band rehearsal. I didn’t realize it was a recruiter until I picked up.

For my own notes (and in case I start second‑guessing everything at 2 a.m. again), here’s what he said:

– Role: “Plant Engineering & Operations Director” – basically a hybrid of what I’ve been doing in operations and what I used to love on the design side.
– Company: Lakeshore Precision Systems, mid‑sized automation outfit in Auburn Hills.
– They liked that I’ve spent the last decade in automotive tooling and that I’ve been managing a small engineering group while still staying close to the shop floor.
– He kept emphasizing that they want someone who can mentor younger engineers and handle customer fire drills. I told him that’s pretty much my life already.

I told him I’m not actively looking and that I’m pretty invested where I am, but I’d at least look at a job description. I also mentioned that my plate is already pretty full with church stuff—especially those months back in 2018–2019 when I was the point person for our little recovery fellowship at Grace, meeting every other Tuesday night with the guys working through alcohol and painkiller addictions.

No decisions, just gathering information. I’ll forward you the JD if he sends it. We can talk more Friday after our movie night.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: That recruiter call during dinner

artifacts.content:
Title: Coffee with Mark – talk about role change
Date: 2025-02-11 07:30-08:30
Location: Biggby Coffee, Novi (Grand River & Meadowbrook)
Attendees: Michael Peterson; Mark Jensen

Notes:

Meet with Mark before work to pick his brain about long‑term career trajectory. He’s been in that “Engineering Manager / Operations Lead” hybrid job at his plant for almost five years now and seems to have found a sustainable pace.

Agenda:
– Ask how he transitioned from being a hands‑on mechanical engineer to more of an operations and people‑management role.
– Pros/cons of stepping fully into an “operations director” type position vs. staying in a mixed technical/lead role.
– How he balances work with family and his volunteer commitments at church.
– Get his thoughts on whether my mix of design experience, production scheduling, and shop‑floor troubleshooting would translate if I ever moved to a different company (e.g., another automation supplier or maybe an in‑house engineering department at an OEM).

Personal reminder: mention how draining those months were back in 2018–2019 when I was coordinating the informal recovery fellowship at Grace every other Tuesday at 7:30 p.m. in Classroom 3, tracking who was in AA, who had just relapsed, and who was on probation after OWI convictions. Helpful experience in pastoral care, but I don’t want that level of late‑night responsibility layered on top of an even bigger job.

Bring notebook; be at office by 9:00 a.m. afterward.

metadata.title: Coffee with Mark – talk about role change

contains_negative_info: mentions relapses and OWI convictions of others

artifacts.content:
From: HR Department <careers@lakeshoreprecision.com>
To: Michael Peterson <michael.peterson@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-01-15 10:42
Subject: Follow‑up: Application – Operations & Engineering Manager

Dear Michael,

Thank you again for taking the time to speak with us last week about the Operations & Engineering Manager position at Lakeshore Precision Systems in Auburn Hills.

As we discussed, the role would involve leading a cross‑functional team of mechanical designers, controls engineers, and production staff, with approximately 60% of your time focused on day‑to‑day operations (scheduling, supplier coordination, and quality issues) and 40% on higher‑level project engineering and customer interaction. Your background managing a small engineering group while remaining involved in hands‑on troubleshooting on the shop floor is exactly the blend we’ve found successful in this position.

We were also impressed by your long‑term commitment to mentorship and community service, including your work at your church coordinating a small men’s group and assisting with hospital visits. The hiring panel noted that your examples of walking alongside men struggling with addiction in a faith‑based setting showed a level of patience and discretion that would translate well to leading a diverse technical team.

At this time, however, we have decided to move forward with another candidate whose experience is a slightly closer match to our internal promotion track. This is in no way a reflection on your qualifications; we believe you would be an asset in a similar Operations & Engineering leadership role.

We will keep your résumé on file for future openings. Thank you again for your interest, and we wish you the best in your current position and any future career steps.

Sincerely,

Angela Ruiz
HR Generalist
Lakeshore Precision Systems

metadata.subject: Follow‑up: Application – Operations & Engineering Manager

artifacts.content:
From: Pastor Daniel Schultz <pastor.schultz@gracelutherannovi.org>
To: Michael Peterson <michael.peterson@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-11-03 16:28
Subject: Deacon check‑in and your work situation

Michael,

Thank you again for covering the adult Bible study and the hospital visit yesterday. I continue to be grateful for the way you juggle family, work, and your deacon responsibilities.

You mentioned after service that things at the plant have been especially intense this fall with the new launch and that your role has gradually shifted from pure design work into more of an operations and people‑management mix. I heard you say you’re effectively acting as an “engineering operations lead” these days—coordinating production schedules, handling customer escalations, and still getting pulled into the tougher mechanical problems when the younger engineers are stuck.

I know you’ve been wrestling with whether to formalize that shift by pursuing a full operations manager title somewhere, or to stay where you are and simply accept that your job has become a hybrid. From a pastoral standpoint, my concern is less about the title on your business card and more about whether the demands are sustainable alongside your call as a husband, father, and deacon.

We’ve seen before—especially back in 2018–2019 when you were the point person for the Tuesday night recovery fellowship—that you’re willing to pour yourself out for others, even texting the men late at night when they were most tempted to relapse. That’s admirable, but I don’t want to see you stretched so thin at work that you have nothing left for Laura, the kids, or your own spiritual rest.

Let’s set up a time this month to talk more and pray about it.

In Christ,
Pastor Dan

metadata.subject: Deacon check‑in and your work situation

contains_negative_info: mentions addiction and relapse of others

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Peterson <michael.peterson@gracelutherannovi.org>
To: Rev. Daniel Keller <dkeller@gracelutherannovi.org>
Cc: Deacon Board <deacons@gracelutherannovi.org>
Date: 2025-02-11 07:42 AM
Subject: Deacon File Update for District Review

Pastor Dan,

As you requested for the upcoming district visitation, I’ve pulled together a brief summary of my training and some of the more significant congregational care situations from the past few years so you can drop it into the deacon roster file.

For education, please list my second post‑college program as: “Lay Ministry / Deacon Certification Coursework, 2021” through the Michigan District extension program (weekend intensives at St. Paul, Ann Arbor, plus the online pastoral care modules). That wrapped up right before Advent 2021, which is when we formally shifted my title from elder chair to deacon (Word and Service).

In terms of responsibilities, beyond the regular hospital and shut‑in visits, it might be worth noting that in Fall 2019 I coordinated a confidential reconciliation process at Grace between two long‑time members, Jim and Linda H. After Jim disclosed an ongoing extramarital affair during an elder home visit on 2019‑09‑10, you and the Board asked me to walk with them. Over the next six months I scheduled and attended four joint meetings in your office, helped arrange a referral to the Christian marriage counselor in Livonia we often recommend, and kept careful notes so the elders could pray and discern how to handle Jim’s leadership roles.

If the district needs anything more specific (course syllabi, dates, or a short narrative of that reconciliation work), I can draft something this weekend after the adult Bible class. Just let me know what format they prefer.

In Christ,
Michael

metadata.subject: Deacon File Update for District Review

artifacts.content:
From: Rev. Daniel Schultz <dschultz@gracelutherannovi.org>
To: Michael Peterson <m.peterson73@gmail.com>
Date: 2020-05-11 08:42 AM
Subject: Deacon coursework timeline for fall

Hi Michael,

I finally heard back from the district office regarding your Word and Service training plan. They confirmed that they will accept the theology and pastoral care credits you completed through Grand Valley back in the 1990s as foundational coursework, so you will not need to repeat those.

They are, however, asking that you finish the remaining online modules through Concordia by the end of the 2020–2022 cycle. Practically, that means starting the first unit this September and wrapping up the last one no later than December 2022. They suggested aiming to complete at least two classes per academic year so you’re not cramming everything into one stretch.

To be clear, this is not a second bachelor’s degree, but it will be recorded in your file as advanced lay training in diaconal ministry. When you’ve finished the sequence, the district will issue a certificate noting the year of completion and the total hours of study in pastoral care, worship leadership, and congregational administration.

Let’s plan to sit down in June to map out which courses you’ll take in 2020–2021 and which you’ll leave for 2021–2022. I’d also like to talk about how this fits with your engineering workload and your trips to Kentwood to help your parents.

In Christ,
Pastor Dan

metadata.subject: Deacon coursework timeline for fall

artifacts.content:
From: Concordia Lay Training Office <laytraining@concordiaonline.edu>
To: Michael Peterson <m.peterson73@gmail.com>
Date: 2019-12-03 3:16 PM
Subject: Academic record and anticipated completion date

Dear Mr. Peterson,

Per your request, we have attached an unofficial transcript summarizing your progress in the Lay Ministry / Deacon Formation track. As of the end of the Fall 2019 term, you have completed 18 continuing education units (CEUs) toward the 30 CEUs required for the certificate.

Our records show that you began the program in Winter 2018 and have steadily taken one or two modules per semester. If you maintain your current pace, your anticipated completion date will fall in late 2022 or early 2023, depending on when you schedule the capstone practicum.

Please note: because this is a non-degree program, we do not list a traditional “graduation year” in the same way we do for undergraduate or graduate degrees. Instead, your file will reflect the year the certificate is conferred, along with a notation that the coursework was completed over multiple terms.

If you need a letter for your congregation summarizing your academic standing and projected finish date, reply to this message with the name of your supervising pastor at Grace Lutheran in Novi, MI, and we will be happy to prepare one.

Blessings on your continued studies and service.

Sincerely,

Karen Holtz
Program Coordinator, Lay Ministry / Deacon Formation
Concordia Online

metadata.subject: Academic record and anticipated completion date

artifacts.content:
Title: Concordia Online – Orientation Webinar (Lay Ministry Track)
Date: 2018-01-22 7:30 PM–9:00 PM
Location: Home Office – Willow Creek Dr., Novi
Attendees: Michael Peterson; Concordia Online Advising Team

Description:

Zoom link: [redacted]

Agenda:
- Overview of the Lay Ministry / Deacon Formation sequence
- Difference between degree programs and certificate/continuing ed tracks
- How CEUs convert to hours for district recognition
- Typical timeline (most students complete in 3–5 years)
- Q&A on balancing coursework with full-time employment

Notes (Michael):

• They emphasized this is NOT a second college degree and won’t show up like “B.A. 1995” or “M.Div. 2004” on transcripts. It’s listed as continuing education, with the completion year only.

• Average student takes 2–3 modules a year. If I only take one per term because of work at Midwest Tooling and the church deacon responsibilities, I’m probably looking at finishing somewhere around 2022 or 2023.

• Advisor said I should keep my original engineering graduation year on any forms that ask for “college graduation year” and only list this program under “additional training” or “certifications,” without trying to match it to a specific college graduation date.

• Need to double‑check with Pastor Dan how the district wants the completion year reported on the congregational records when I’m done.

artifacts.content:

To: Michael Peterson <m.peterson73@gmail.com>
Date: 2023-01-09 9:02 PM
Subject: Updating your bio for the church website

Hey Michael,

I just finished proofing the draft of your deacon bio that Sarah wants to post on the Grace Lutheran website. She left a blank where the office was asking for a “second college graduation year,” and I wasn’t sure what you wanted to put there.

Right now it lists your mechanical engineering degree from Michigan Tech with the correct year, and then it mentions your ongoing deacon training through Concordia. Since the lay ministry program isn’t really a traditional college degree, I suggested she just write “current lay ministry coursework through Concordia Online” instead of trying to force a specific graduation year.

If you’d rather give them an estimated completion year for the certificate (you’d mentioned maybe wrapping things up in 2022 or 2023), you can email Sarah directly and let her know. Just be careful not to make it sound like you have a second bachelor’s or a master’s—some people might misunderstand.

Also, she asked if you want to include anything about your parents’ situation in Kentwood under the “areas of interest” section (e.g., caregiving, supporting aging parents). Totally up to you, but I thought it might help others feel less alone.

Love you,
Laura

Post‑service meeting to talk through:

artifacts.content:
From: admissions@ctsfw.edu
To: Michael Peterson <mapeterson73@gmail.com>
Date: 2020-02-11 08:42 AM
Subject: Confirmation of Completion – Lay Ministry Certificate (2019 Cohort)

Dear Mr. Peterson,

Congratulations again on successfully completing the Lay Ministry Certificate program through Concordia Theological Seminary’s distance education track.

Our records show that you began the certificate sequence in Fall 2017 and fulfilled all required coursework and supervised ministry components by December 2019. Your official completion date has been recorded as December 15, 2019, and the certificate was conferred at the Winter 2020 meeting of the faculty.

For your files, the program is listed as:

Program: Lay Ministry / Deacon Formation Certificate
Completion year: 2019
Delivery: Online with local congregation supervision (Grace Lutheran Church, Novi, MI)

Please note that this is a non‑degree, post‑baccalaureate certificate and should be listed separately from your formal mechanical engineering degree on any resumes or professional documents. A common format would be:

“Lay Ministry / Deacon Formation Certificate – Concordia Theological Seminary, Fort Wayne, IN (completed 2019)”

If you require an official transcript or a letter verifying your 2019 completion for your congregation, district office, or employer, you may request it through the student services portal. Standard processing time is 7–10 business days.

Thank you for your faithful work in preparation for service in Word and Service ministry. Please extend our greetings to Rev. Schultz and the congregation at Grace.

In Christ,

Rev. Dr. Alan Krueger
Director of Distance Education
Concordia Theological Seminary, Fort Wayne

metadata.subject: Confirmation of Completion – Lay Ministry Certificate (2019 Cohort)

artifacts.content:
From: Pastor Jonathan Schultz <jschultz@gracelutherannovi.org>
To: Michael Peterson <michael.peterson@midwesttooling.com>
Date: 2018-09-05 6:27 AM
Subject: Biographical blurb for district deacon training application

Mike,

I’m filling out the district paperwork to enroll you in the next phase of deacon training, and they’re asking for a brief educational history. I drafted the following—can you look it over and tell me if the years are right?

“Michael A. Peterson (b. 1973) is a long‑time member of Grace Lutheran, Novi. He holds a B.S. in Mechanical Engineering from Michigan Technological University (graduated 1996). He later completed a congregational lay leadership course through the Michigan District in 2014 and began deacon formation coursework in 2017, with anticipated completion in 2020. Michael currently serves as an elder and Bible study leader.”

If I remember correctly, you wrapped up that Michigan District lay leadership series the same year Jacob started high school (2014), and you didn’t jump into the formal deacon coursework until a few years later. I put 2020 as the expected completion year for the deacon classes since that’s what we discussed with the district office last spring.

Let me know by tonight if any of those dates are off before I submit the form. Also, if you want to add anything about your engineering background or continuing education at work (Six Sigma, project management seminars, etc.), I can squeeze in one more sentence.

Peace,

Pastor Jon

Michael A. Peterson
24179 Willow Creek Dr

If this address is still accurate, no action is required. If you have recently moved or prefer to receive your packet at a different address (for example, a P.O. Box or alternate residence), please reply to this email with your updated information by Friday, October 13.

Please do NOT send Social Security numbers or banking details via email. If you need to update those items, log in to the Employee Self‑Service portal or contact HR directly at 248‑555‑0194.

Note: The address on file here is used for benefits correspondence only and does not affect your payroll direct deposit or tax withholding elections.

Thank you for your prompt attention to this matter, and for your continued service at Midwest Tooling & Automation.

Best regards,

Kathy Reynolds
HR/Benefits Coordinator
Midwest Tooling & Automation

metadata.subject: Please confirm mailing address for open enrollment packet

artifacts.content:
Title: Meeting with financial planner – update address & beneficiaries
Date: 2024-07-18 16:00–17:00
Location: Fidelity Office – 39500 Orchard Hill Pl, Novi, MI 48375
Attendees: Michael Peterson; Laura Peterson; Jim Wallace (advisor)

Notes:

Agenda:
1. Review retirement projections and college funding for Jacob & Nathan.
2. Confirm contact info, including home address for statements.
3. Discuss long‑term care policy options for Michael’s parents.

Prep notes (Michael):
- Bring latest pay stub and 401(k) statement.
- Double‑check that they have our current address listed as:
   Michael & Laura Peterson
   24781 W. Willow Creek Drive
   Novi, MI 48375
- Ask Jim whether it’s better to keep paper statements going to the house or switch everything to online only. I’m a little uneasy about so much financial mail sitting in the mailbox when we’re out of town visiting Emily in Chicago.

Follow‑up tasks:
- Email Emily and Jacob with high‑level summary (NOT dollar amounts) so they know we’re trying to plan ahead.
- Talk with Mom and Dad about whether they’d consider a small long‑term care policy, or if that ship has sailed given Dad’s stroke last year.
- Confirm with Jim that our church (Grace Lutheran, Novi) is still listed as a contingent beneficiary on the small life policy from my old job in Grand Rapids.

Reminder: leave work by 3:15 to avoid I‑696 traffic and swing by home to pick up Laura.

metadata.title: Meeting with financial planner – update address & beneficiaries

artifacts.content:
Title: City of Novi inspection – verify house number & mailbox
Date: 2023-09-14 10:30–11:30
Location: Peterson residence – driveway/front yard
Attendees: Michael Peterson; City of Novi Code Enforcement Officer

Description:

Follow‑up visit from the city regarding the complaint about faded curb numbers and the leaning mailbox post on our street. Officer said it’s not a formal violation yet, but they’re asking homeowners along Willow Creek to make sure addresses are clearly visible for emergency vehicles.

Notes to self:
- Our current numbering on the house reads “24691 W Willow Creek” in those old brass digits the previous owner put up. They’re hard to see at night.
- Mailbox has the same 24691 number on only one side. Need to ask if they want it on both sides or if reflective stickers are enough.
- Double‑check with the officer whether they have our lot recorded correctly in the city system; tax bill still shows the parcel as "Lot 18 – Willow Creek Estates" with the 24691 address.

Action items after visit:
- Stop by Home Depot for new black metal numbers and a solar light for the front step.
- Text Laura a photo of possible mailbox replacements.
- Update our address in the Novi Online Services portal if they recommend any formatting changes (directional, abbreviations, etc.).

Reminder: move the Accord out of the driveway so the inspector can pull in.

metadata.title: City of Novi inspection – verify house number & mailbox
attendees: City of Novi Code Enforcement Officer

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Peterson <laura.peterson@gmail.com>
To: Michael Peterson <mapeterson73@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-01-06 21:37
Subject: Old Grand Rapids address for Nathan’s soccer registration

Hey Michael,

I’m filling out the online form for Nathan’s indoor soccer league and it’s asking for “previous address (if moved within last 10 years).” Do you remember exactly how it was written when we lived in Grand Rapids?

I dug out one of the old mortgage statements from the file box in the basement. It lists our address as:

    1345 Brookside Court SE
    Grand Rapids, MI 49508

That’s what I’m planning to type in unless you remember it differently. Funny to think we brought Emily home from the hospital to that little ranch house. Hard to believe it’s been more than 15 years since we left Grand Rapids for Novi.

The form also wants our “current mailing address,” but it looks like that’s just for league correspondence, so I might put the P.O. Box we used temporarily when the house was being painted last summer:

    P.O. Box 771
    Novi, MI 48376

That way we don’t have flyers and junk piling up on the porch again.

Can you double‑check these when you get a minute? I’d like to submit everything tonight so Nathan doesn’t get stuck on the waitlist.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Old Grand Rapids address for Nathan’s soccer registration

artifacts.content:
Title: UPS Hold Mail Pickup – Novi
Date: 2024-08-03 09:30–10:00
Location: UPS Customer Center, 12500 Merriman Rd, Livonia, MI 48150

Notes:
Vacation mail pickup after Chicago trip to see Emily.

Details:

Hold mail was set up for the townhouse address we used during the brief renovation this summer:

    41102 Willow Bend Townhomes, Unit 7
    Novi, MI 48375

We forwarded most of the important stuff there while the main house was torn up (siding, windows, and that surprise soffit repair). UPS ended up holding a couple of packages and a stack of forwarded USPS mail that didn’t get delivered before we drove to Chicago.

To bring:
- Driver’s license with the Novi address we updated at the Secretary of State last year
- Utility bill from DTE for the Willow Bend unit (shows our name and temporary residence)

Laura wants to stop at Meijer on the way back to restock the fridge at the main house, since we basically emptied it before leaving. Nathan has practice in the afternoon, so aim to be home by 12:30.

Reminder: After picking everything up, sort through and make sure any medical bills for Dad’s stroke follow‑up get filed in the Kentwood folder, and anything related to the townhouse lease goes in the "Willow Bend" envelope in the desk drawer.

metadata.location: UPS Customer Center, 12500 Merriman Rd, Livonia, MI 48150

Location: Keller Williams Grand Rapids, 1555 Arboretum Dr SE, Grand Rapids, MI 49546

Notes:
Drive over from Novi early (leave by 8:15 a.m.) to meet with realtor about possible downsized ranch or condo for Mom and Dad.

Realtor: Sarah Jensen – recommended by Bob’s neighbor.

Addresses to review (print listings ahead of time):

1) 2871 Mapleview Drive SW
   Kentwood, MI 49508

2) 4420 Eastbrook Meadows Ct SE, Unit 12
   Kentwood, MI 49512

3) 1900 Breton Woods Dr SE, Apt 104
   Grand Rapids, MI 49506

Bring copies of:
- Current lease for their Kentwood duplex at 2210 Ridgecrest Lane SE
- Last DTE and Consumers Energy bills (showing mailing address and account numbers)
- Notes from Dad’s stroke follow‑up about stairs and mobility concerns

After the meeting, swing by their duplex to walk through and measure furniture. Laura wants pictures of the living room and bedroom so she can plan what to keep and what to donate.

Reminder: Do NOT give Sarah our Novi home address yet; just let her know we’re local family helping from the east side of the state. We’ll share contact info once Mom and Dad are ready to list the duplex or sign anything.
